text: Considering the nodule size and that the lingula was not affected, we decided to perform a trisegmentectomy, sparing the lingula.
We divide the pulmonary ligament and identify the inferior pulmonary vein.
Now we look for the superior pulmonary vein.
Now we dissect the pulmonary artery in the orthopulmonary window.
We remove all the nodes we find in this region.
We return to the superior pulmonary vein and identify its two branches.
The trisegmental vein is the uppermost tributary of the superior pulmonary vein.
We encircle it
and divide it by using an endo-stapler, preserving the lingual vein.
When dissecting the pulmonary artery, the first branch we find is the apical posterior one.
Its dissection must be extremely careful.
because an injury here could be difficult to control.
Excessive tension must be avoided when transacting it with an endostabler.
The next branch we find is the superior segmental one.
The branches is behind the superior pulmonary vein stamp.
Now we expose the segmental bifurcation.
We encircle the trisegmental branch with care to avoid injury in the artery.
Before transecting it, we clamp it and insufflate the lung to ensure that the lingula is not compromised.
CPAP can be useful to identify the theoretical physio and now we divide it from anterior to posterior by using endo-staplers.
The specimen is removed in a bag and finally we complete the lymphadenectomy.
